DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Feb-28-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by Zoom):
Issa Azat, Esq. 626-696-3400 The Azat Law Group for Plaintiff.
John Grele, Esq. 415-517-0395 Law Office of John Grele for Defendant.

ARGUED. Mr. Grele contest and argues. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

Defendants' demurrer to all four causes of action in the Second Amended Complaint is overruled.

"In reviewing the sufficiency of a complaint against a general demurrer, we are guided by long-settled rules. 'We treat the demurrer as admitting all material facts properly pleaded, but not contentions, deductions or conclusions of fact or law. [Citation.] We also consider matters which may be judicially noticed.'" (Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318; Donabedian v. Mercury Ins. Co. (2004) 116 Cal.App.4th 968, 994 ["In reviewing the ruling on a demurrer, a court cannot consider the substance of declarations, matter not subject to judicial notice, or documents judicially noticed but not accepted for the truth of their contents."].) "The purpose of a general demurrer is to determine the sufficiency of the complaint and the court should only rule on matters disclosed in that pleading." (Ion Equpment Corporation v. Nelson (1980) 110 Cal.App.3d 868, 881.)

Defendants' demurrer argues that LegalMatch has a new board of directors and plaintiff must therefore make a new demand or plead futility. (See Apple Inc. v. Superior Court (2017) 18 Cal.App.5th 222.) This argument is predicated on the declaration of counsel, which is improper on demurrer. In Apple Inc. v. Superior Court (2017) 18 Cal.App.5th 222, 241, the moving defendant established the composition of the new Apple board of directors through judicially noticeable regulatory filings.

Order is signed.

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Compensatory And Punitive Damages Claims For Plaintiff; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support; Declaration Of John R Grele
Off Calendar Nov-20-2024 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS DMITRY SHUBOV and VLADIMIR SHUBOV's Motion To Strike Compensatory And Punitive Damages Claims For Plaintiff.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

The motion to strike is granted with 20 days leave to amend.

At the last hearing involving the original complaint, the court noted that the allegations regarding the $3 million dollar transfer implicated a corporate injury and gave plaintiff leave to amend to plead a basis to compel a dividend or cite other injury "not incidental to an injury to the corporation." (Jones v. H.F. Ahmanson & Co. (1969) 1 Cal.3d 93, 107 ["If the injury is not incidental to an injury to the corporation, an individual cause of action exists."].) The first amended complaint does not plead a basis for direct recovery and plaintiff apparently now only seeks to allege derivative causes of action.

But the first amended complaint still seeks to recover for a direct/personal injury to plaintiff. (First Amended Complaint, pars. 37, 39, 45, 47.) Indeed, plaintiff filed the first amended complaint as "an individual" (not a proxy/representative of the corporation) even though she is prosecuting derivative claims. (Corporations Code section 800(b) [conditions to maintain derivative action].) The adduced allegations are improper as plaintiff alleges no basis to recover direct compensatory or punitive damages. Only Legalmatch.com can recover such damages. Plaintiff has leave to amend to allege the derivative claims in a proper format.

Order signed.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT VLADIMIR SHUBOV's MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Vladimir Shubov ("Vladimir") seeks to strike the following:

1. Page 9, Line 46, which states "The aforementioned conduct of defendants was the
product of a willful and conscious disregard for the fiduciary obligations owed to
LM and to its property and legal rights, performed with the intent or reckless
disregard to cause injury to LM, and was despicable conduct that subjected LM to a
hardship in conscious disregard of LM's rights so as to justify an award of
exemplary and punitive damages."
2. Page 9, Line 47, which states "By reason of defendant's willful, fraudulent,
oppressive, and malicious conduct, Ostrovsky seeks to recover punitive or
exemplary damages."
3. Page 11-12, Lines 65-69, which states Plaintiff's Sixth cause of action against Defendant.
4. Page 12, Lines 70-72, which states Plaintiff's Seventh cause of action against
Defendant.
5. Page 12, Plaintiff's Second prayer for relief for punitive damages.
6. Page 13, Plaintiff's Fifth prayer for relief for attorney's fees.
7. Page 13, Plaintiff's Sixth prayer for relief for declaratory and injunctive relief in the form of an award granting Plaintiff the relief requested in the Complaint.

The motion is denied as to items one and two and four through seven. Plaintiff alleges sufficient facts to support the claims for punitive damages and injunctive relief. As explained, plaintiff's derivative claims are well pleaded. The motion is moot as to item three because the court is dismissing the declaratory relief claim.

Counsel Michael Azat is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling.

MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Feb-20-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. MICHAEL AZAT, Esq. of The Azat Law Group (Tel: 626-696-3400) for Plaintiff.
Atty. JOHN GRELE, Esq. of Law Office of John R. Grele PC (Tel: 415-655-8776) for Defendants.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT DMITRY SHUBOV's MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Dmitry Shubov's ("Dmitry") motion to strike is granted and denied in part.

Dmitry seeks to strike the following:

1. Page 3, Line 12, which states "after it was founded until he pleaded guilty in federal court to a federal felony and his New York law licenses was revoked."
2. Page 3, Line 13, which states "Shubov, who was prone to eccentricities"
3. Page 4, Line 14, which states "In 2017, Shubov ran into more legal trouble when he was charged with felony counts in Stanislaus County, California, related to a sexual relationship with a minor. Shubov once again pled guilty to a felony and received a term of probation."
4. Page 7, Line 35, which states "Shubov's conduct justifies an award of punitive damages because he acted with malice, oppression, and fraud."
5. Page 8, Line 39, which states "Shubov violated his fiduciary duties when he abused his position with LM and engaged in self-dealing. Shubov was aware of the damage that it would cause to LM's brand and goodwill if the public and potential customers learned that LM's Board was dominated by Shubov and that Shubov exerted control over LM's daily operations and management. As a result, Shubov agreed to step down from his position as LM's Chief Executive Officer and instead vested control of LM to an objective Board."
6. Page 9, Line 46, which states "The aforementioned conduct of defendants was the product of a willful and conscious disregard for the fiduciary obligations owed to LM and to its property and legal rights, performed with the intent or reckless disregard to cause injury to LM, and was despicable conduct that subjected LM to a hardship in conscious disregard of LM's rights so as to justify an award of
exemplary and punitive damages."
7. Page 9, Line 47, which states "By reason of defendant's willful, fraudulent,
oppressive, and malicious conduct, Ostrovsky seeks to recover punitive or
exemplary damages."
8. Page 10-11, Lines 56-60, which states Plaintiff's Fourth cause of action against Defendant.
9. Page 11, Lines 61-64, which states Plaintiff's Fifth cause of action against
Defendant.
10. Page 11-12, Lines 65-69, which states Plaintiff's Sixth cause of action against Defendant.
11. Page 12, Lines 70-72, which states Plaintiff's Seventh cause of action against Defendant.
12. Page 12, Plaintiff's Second prayer for relief for punitive damages.
13. Page 13, Plaintiff's Fifth prayer for relief for attorney's fees.
14. Page 13, Plaintiff's Sixth prayer for relief for declaratory and injunctive relief in the form of an award granting Plaintiff the relief requested in the Complaint.

The motion is granted without leave to amend as to items one, two and three. The allegations regarding Dmitry's prior legal troubles and his "eccentricities" are irrelevant and unnecessary to plaintiff's claims. (CCP 436(a).) The motion is moot as to item ten since the court is dismissing cause of action six. The remainder of the motion is denied. Plaintiff pleads sufficient facts to support an award of punitive damages and the adduced claims and prayers for relief are well-pleaded.

Counsel Michael Azat is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not Reported. (302/RBU)
